subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Senate Filibuster: Beneficial or Detrimental to Policy Making
stimulus: There is debate over the use of the Senate filibuster in the national legislative process. Take a position on whether the filibuster is beneficial or detrimental to policy making in the United States Congress.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Article I of the United States Constitution, The Federalist 10, The Federalist 51.)
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position on whether the filibuster is beneficial or detrimental. Generic statements about the filibuster being used do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The filibuster is detrimental to policy making because it leads to gridlock and the American people need the federal government to act and pass laws for the general welfare.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The filibuster is detrimental because it makes the work of Congress less efficient.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The filibuster is beneficial because it limits the power of the federal government to create policies that would be unpopular in some parts of the country.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Lawmaking in the Senate benefits from the filibuster because it fosters compromise.”

wrong_examples: “There is a debate over the use of the Senate filibuster in the legislative process.” Do not respond to the prompt
“The Senate filibuster is often used during the policy making process.” Responses that earn this point:
Respond to the prompt rather than restating or rephrasing the prompt and establish a line of reasoning.
Provide a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning about whether the filibuster is beneficial or detrimental to policy making in the United States Congress.
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, must have earned Row A. To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must come from Article I, Federalist 10, or Federalist 51.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

required_elements: No accurate or relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain the connection between evidence and the claim.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I never mentions the filibuster, instead allowing a majority vote to pass policy in both chambers of Congress. So, the use of the filibuster to stop a democratically elected majority from making policy is detrimental to policy making because it is undemocratic and inconsistent with the Framers’ intent.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I allows the chambers to make their own rules, therefore, the Senate chose to create the filibuster. It is beneficial for policymaking because it ensures that minority views can influence policy, which can lead to compromise and more moderate policies.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Fed 10 states that factions in a large republic will balance each other. Fed 10 supports the idea that the filibuster is detrimental to policy making because it advantages one faction over others. A faction of senators can block policy that a majority supports and undermines policymaking.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Fed 10 explains that factions are part of human nature. The filibuster allows a faction of senators to protect us against tyranny of the majority, benefitting the policy making.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Fed 51 describes the importance of checks and balances and supports the idea that the filibuster is beneficial to policy making because it can be used to block Supreme Court nominees who would strike down policies that benefit the country.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “The filibuster moves beyond the separation of powers described in Fed 51 and becomes detrimental to policy making because it can stop the president from seeing their policies enacted even when a majority of Congress and the public favor the policies.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “It could be argued that the filibuster benefits policy making because it ensures

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “It is simply wrong to state that the filibuster is necessary as a check on limited government which was the intent of the framers, but the filibuster is power of majorities.” detrimental because there are many issues that cannot be adequately addressed in a Congress limited by the filibuster.” Describe an alternate perspective but do not refute, concede, or rebut that perspective

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Opponents of the filibuster argue that it prevents the majority party from passing popular bills.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Opponents of the filibuster believe it is bad for policy making because they argue that we need a more efficient process for passing laws at the national level, but removing the filibuster reduces the likelihood that the policymaking process is truly democratic.”

common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
